title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Colonel Barry, Miss Bustle, Mrs. Gardner, Mr. Gardner, Hercule Poirot, and the vicar, Stephen Lane, engage in a conversation regarding modern social habits and the nature of the seaside. Mrs. Gardner discusses the casual nature of modern youth and expresses her concern that her daughter, Erin, might grow excessive body hair if she sunbathes too much. Poirot compares the sight of bodies lined up to a mortuary or meat on a butcher's slab, a comparison that draws indignation from Mrs. Gardner.

As the Gardners depart for the hotel, Stephen Lane joins the group. The conversation shifts to physical activities, such as walking and rowing, and the physiological causes of seasickness and acrophobia. Miss Bustle mentions that Mrs. Redfern suffers from severe dizziness at heights. Christine Redfern joins the group after swimming, noting that her pale skin does not tan but instead develops rashes. Miss Bustle suggests that Poirot play a joke on Mrs. Gardner by telling her a murderer is staying at the hotel, but Poirot notes she would likely believe him.

The discussion turns to the likelihood of crime in such a peaceful location. While Miss Bustle believes the area is too tranquil for murder, Poirot argues that evil exists everywhere, even under the sun. He explains that the seaside provides a perfect cover for enemies to meet, as the tradition of August vacations makes the presence of any individual appear natural and inconspicuous. Stephen Lane concludes by reflecting on Poirot's words, citing the concept that the human heart is full of evil and madness.
